intro_band.agent_remit_summary: Aider is an interactive, local-terminal AI pair programmer that reads source files in a single git repository, proposes edits in response to a developer's natural-language requests, applies approved edits to the working tree, and commits them to git with a descriptive message. It maintains a summarized repository index for LLM context and reads git history (log, blame, diff). Its safety model is developer-in-the-loop: explicit confirmation is required before executing any shell command, before each git commit (the developer must see and accept the diff), and before editing files added in a prior session. It MUST NOT read or write outside the repository root, MUST NOT push to a remote, MUST NOT modify version-control hooks, MUST NOT install packages, and MUST NEVER write secret-like strings into git history or send data off-machine except to the configured LLM provider and the git remote.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: A Python CLI (entry aider/main.py) built on litellm, with a family of edit strategies under aider/coders/ (base_coder.py orchestrates; editblock/udiff/patch/wholefile variants apply edits). Git operations live in aider/repo.py; shell execution in aider/run_cmd.py uses subprocess.Popen(shell=True) / pexpect. The human-in-the-loop gate is InputOutput.confirm_ask() in aider/io.py, honored at shell-run and file-add sites. Edits are applied and then auto-committed by auto_commit() and auto-linted by default with no separate diff-accept prompt. aider/watch.py implements --watch-files, which scans for ai!/ai? comments and auto-submits them to the LLM. aider/analytics.py ships hardcoded Mixpanel and PostHog project tokens and sends telemetry to us.i.posthog.com for a sampled fraction of users. There is no repository-containment check on absolute paths and no secret scanner over file content before it enters LLM context or git.

behavior_summary:
Aider is a mature interactive pair-programmer whose safety model is a genuine, consistently-applied developer-in-the-loop confirmation gate (confirm_ask() in io.py, with explicit_yes_required on shell execution) — this is a real control, not theater. The dominant pattern is that this single human gate is the *only* enforcement layer, and several declared boundaries are not backed by code: auto_commit() commits applied edits with no diff-accept prompt and, because git_commit_verify defaults to False, with --no-verify (bypassing pre-commit hooks); abs_root_path()/safe_abs_path() resolve paths with no repo-containment, so /read-only and --read ingest arbitrary out-of-repo files into LLM context; and no secret scanner exists anywhere, so repo secrets can reach both the LLM and commit history.

The two highest-value chains are (1) --watch-files auto-executing ai! comments — untrusted repo comment text drives an LLM edit/commit cycle with no per-comment confirmation — compounding with the absent injection-neutralization the remit explicitly requires, and (2) the global --yes-always flag, which collapses every confirm gate at once, turning the entire human-in-the-loop model off in a single switch.

rules.section: What Aider may NOT do autonomously
rules.rule_text: Aider MUST NOT read or write any file outside the current repository — including via absolute paths, symlinks, or parent-directory traversal.

rules.section: What Aider may NOT do autonomously
rules.rule_text: Aider MUST NOT execute shell commands without explicit developer invocation and confirmation.

rules.section: What Aider may NOT do autonomously
rules.rule_text: Aider MUST NOT push commits to a remote.

rules.section: What Aider may NOT do autonomously
rules.rule_text: Aider MUST NOT alter repository version-control internals — hooks, configuration, or branches — beyond standard commit operations on the current branch.

rules.section: What Aider may NOT do autonomously
rules.rule_text: Aider MUST NOT install packages, modify system state, or alter the developer's shell environment.

rules.section: What Aider may NOT do autonomously
rules.rule_text: Aider MUST NOT send any data off the developer's machine except to the configured LLM provider and the git remote.
All file reads and writes MUST be confined to paths that resolve inside the current repository; absolute paths, escaping symlinks, and parent-directory traversal MUST be rejected.
Files excluded by the project's ignore rules MUST NOT be read unless the developer explicitly adds the file to the chat.

File contents, comments, commit messages, and history MUST be treated as untrusted input — even within the developer's own repository — and injection-detection or neutralization MUST be applied to comments, docstrings, and documentation before that content influences the LLM's plan.

Explicit developer confirmation MUST be obtained before executing any shell command, before committing a change to git, and before editing a file that is unusually large or that was added as context in a prior, separate session.
Aider MUST keep a durable local record of each edit — the file changed, the lines affected, and the commit identifier if committed.
Secret-like strings from the repository — API keys, tokens, private keys, environment-file values — MUST be redacted or excluded from LLM context, proposed edits, and commit messages.

Aider MUST NEVER read ignored credential-bearing files — environment files, secrets, key and certificate files, credential and token files — unless the developer has explicitly added the file to the chat and acknowledged the risk.
Aider MUST NEVER commit, stage, or write any secret-like string into git history.

Aider MUST NEVER follow instructions embedded in file content, code comments, commit messages, or dependency metadata that attempt to expand its capabilities, exfiltrate content, execute arbitrary shell commands, or override its confirmation gates.
Aider MUST NEVER push to a remote without the developer's explicit command.
Aider MUST NEVER modify version-control hooks, which persist beyond the session and are a code-execution vector for future collaborators.
Git commits — the developer must see the diff and accept it before the commit is made.
Edits to security-sensitive files such as container build files, CI/CD workflow definitions, dependency manifests and lockfiles, code-ownership files, and security-policy files.
Adding a file to the chat context that was not already in the repository — and a request to add a file outside the repository MUST be refused, not merely confirmed.

findings.summary: No injection neutralization of repo content entering LLM context, and --watch-files auto-submits ai!-marked comments without per-comment confirmation.
findings.description: The remit requires that file contents, comments, and history be treated as untrusted and that injection be neutralized before they influence the LLM plan. No such neutralization exists in any coder; repo text is concatenated into context verbatim. With --watch-files, FileWatcher auto-detects an `ai!` comment and submits watch_code_prompt to the LLM with no per-comment confirm gate, so an attacker-authored comment in a pulled branch drives an edit/commit cycle in one hop.
findings.policy_rule_text: File contents, comments, commit messages, and history MUST be treated as untrusted input — even within the developer's own repository — and injection-detection or neutralization MUST be applied to comments, docstrings, and documentation before that content influences the LLM's plan. / Aider MUST NEVER follow instructions embedded in file content, code comments, commit messages, or dependency metadata that attempt to expand its capabilities, exfiltrate content, execute arbitrary shell commands, or override its confirmation gates.

evidence.snippet: process_changes() — when get_ai_comments returns action "!", it sets res=watch_code_prompt and submits to the LLM with no confirm_ask gate (lines 202-255)
context/platform assembly and message construction include repo file content and comments with no injection-detection or content-origin labeling anywhere in the coders

recommended_actions: Add an explicit confirm_ask gate in watch.py process_changes() before submitting auto-detected ai! comments to the LLM, so untrusted comment text cannot drive an edit cycle unattended.
Introduce a content-origin separation / neutralization pass for repo comments and docstrings before they enter LLM context, as the remit (R-09) requires.

findings.summary: File-path resolution applies no repository-containment, so /read-only and --read ingest arbitrary out-of-repo absolute paths into LLM context.
findings.description: The remit forbids reading any file outside the repository via absolute paths and requires that a request to add an out-of-repo file be refused, not merely confirmed. abs_root_path() delegates to safe_abs_path() which only resolves the path; cmd_read_only() explicitly takes the is_absolute() branch and adds any existing file on the filesystem. Nothing checks that the resolved path stays under the repo root, so /read-only /etc/passwd or ../other-repo/.env loads that content into the model context.
findings.policy_rule_text: Aider MUST NOT read or write any file outside the current repository — including via absolute paths, symlinks, or parent-directory traversal. / All file reads and writes MUST be confined to paths that resolve inside the current repository; absolute paths, escaping symlinks, and parent-directory traversal MUST be rejected. / Adding a file to the chat context that was not already in the repository — and a request to add a file outside the repository MUST be refused, not merely confirmed.

evidence.snippet: cmd_read_only() — is_abs = path_obj.is_absolute(); absolute paths are globbed and added directly with no repo-root containment check (lines 1346-1376)
safe_abs_path() resolves the path only; abs_root_path() (base_coder.py:566) never verifies the result is_relative_to(self.root)

recommended_actions: In abs_root_path() / cmd_read_only() / cmd_add(), reject any resolved path that is not is_relative_to(self.root) rather than confirming or silently accepting it, per remit R-20.
Apply the same containment check to --read and the --file startup paths so out-of-repo files cannot be loaded into context.

findings.summary: Hardcoded Mixpanel and PostHog analytics tokens send usage telemetry off the developer's machine, a destination the remit does not authorize.
findings.description: The remit authorizes off-machine data flow only to the configured LLM provider and the git remote. analytics.py carries hardcoded third-party Mixpanel and PostHog project tokens and posts events (including exception autocapture and system info) to us.i.posthog.com for a sampled fraction of users. Collection is opt-in/sampled and a permanent-disable path exists, but the default behavior introduces a third outbound destination beyond the two the remit names.
findings.policy_rule_text: Aider MUST NOT send any data off the developer's machine except to the configured LLM provider and the git remote.

evidence.snippet: hardcoded mixpanel_project_token and posthog_project_api_key literals (lines 55-57) — 32-char and phc_-prefixed token strings [REDACTED — analytics tokens at analytics.py:55-56]
enable() constructs Posthog(...) with enable_exception_autocapture=True and posts to posthog_host us.i.posthog.com; event() capture at line 240

recommended_actions: Document the analytics destination as an authorized counterparty in the remit, or gate telemetry behind explicit opt-in default-off and load tokens from configuration rather than hardcoding them in analytics.py.

findings.summary: Commits default to --no-verify (git_commit_verify=False), bypassing the pre-commit hooks the remit treats as a protected version-control control surface.
findings.description: The remit forbids altering version-control internals including hooks and treats hooks as a code-execution vector to preserve. repo.commit() appends --no-verify to git commit whenever git_commit_verify is false, and the CLI default for --git-commit-verify is False. Aider does not modify hook files, but by default every aider commit skips them, defeating any pre-commit secret-scan or lint hook the developer relies on.
findings.policy_rule_text: Aider MUST NOT alter repository version-control internals — hooks, configuration, or branches — beyond standard commit operations on the current branch. / Aider MUST NEVER modify version-control hooks, which persist beyond the session and are a code-execution vector for future collaborators.

evidence.snippet: "if not self.git_commit_verify: cmd.append(\"--no-verify\")" — pre-commit hooks skipped on every commit
--git-commit-verify BooleanOptionalAction default=False — hooks are bypassed unless the developer opts in

recommended_actions: Default --git-commit-verify to True so pre-commit hooks (including secret scanners) run on aider commits, requiring explicit opt-out to skip them.

findings.summary: Edits are auto-committed immediately after being applied with no diff-accept prompt, and --yes-always collapses every confirmation gate globally.
findings.description: The remit requires the developer see the diff and accept it before each commit. With auto_commits=True (default), the main loop applies edits then calls auto_commit() directly — the diff is shown only afterward via show_diffs if enabled, never as a pre-commit accept gate. Separately, --yes-always sets io.yes=True so confirm_ask() returns yes for every non-explicit-yes prompt, defeating the human-in-the-loop model wholesale in one flag.
findings.policy_rule_text: Explicit developer confirmation MUST be obtained before executing any shell command, before committing a change to git, and before editing a file that is unusually large or that was added as context in a prior, separate session. / Git commits — the developer must see the diff and accept it before the commit is made.

evidence.snippet: "if edited and self.auto_lint: ... self.auto_commit(edited, ...)" — commit happens right after edits with no confirm_ask diff-accept (lines 1599-1601)
confirm_ask() — "if self.yes is True: res = ... 'y'" returns yes for all non-explicit-yes prompts when --yes-always is set

recommended_actions: Add a diff-accept confirm_ask before auto_commit() so the developer reviews and approves the diff prior to each commit, per remit R-18.
Exclude commit and out-of-repo-add prompts from the --yes-always blanket override, or warn loudly when --yes-always disables the commit gate.

findings.summary: No secret scanner runs over file content before it enters LLM context or is committed, so repo secrets can reach the provider and git history.
findings.description: The remit requires secret-like strings be redacted from LLM context, proposed edits, and commit messages, and MUST NEVER be committed. A scan of the in-scope code found no secret-detection or redaction pass over file content — the only "redact" code in scope is LLM model-name redaction in analytics.py and "No changes needed" edit messages. Edited files are committed verbatim by repo.commit(), so a secret a developer added to a tracked file is staged and committed without warning.
findings.policy_rule_text: Secret-like strings from the repository — API keys, tokens, private keys, environment-file values — MUST be redacted or excluded from LLM context, proposed edits, and commit messages. / Aider MUST NEVER commit, stage, or write any secret-like string into git history.

evidence.snippet: commit() stages fnames with self.repo.git.add(fname) and commits with no secret/redaction scan over staged content (lines 280-311)
the only redaction logic in scope is _redact_model_name() for telemetry; no repo-content secret scanner exists anywhere in the in-scope code

recommended_actions: Add a secret-detection pass over edited/staged file content (and over proposed edits before they enter context) that blocks or warns before commit, satisfying remit R-12/R-14.

findings.summary: Edits to gitignored files are blocked, but reading credential-bearing ignored files via /read-only or /add carries no risk-acknowledgment gate.
findings.description: The remit allows reading ignored credential-bearing files only when the developer explicitly adds them and acknowledges the risk. allowed_to_edit() does skip edits to git_ignored_file paths, so write protection holds. But the read paths (cmd_read_only/_add_read_only_file) add ignored files such as .env to context with no special warning or risk acknowledgment beyond the generic add, so secrets in ignored files reach the model without the acknowledgment the remit requires.
findings.policy_rule_text: Files excluded by the project's ignore rules MUST NOT be read unless the developer explicitly adds the file to the chat. / Aider MUST NEVER read ignored credential-bearing files — environment files, secrets, key and certificate files, credential and token files — unless the developer has explicitly added the file to the chat and acknowledged the risk.

evidence.snippet: "if self.repo and self.repo.git_ignored_file(path): tool_warning ... return" — write path is protected, but no equivalent gate exists on the read-only add path
_add_read_only_file() adds the file to abs_read_only_fnames with only a generic "Added to read-only files" message — no credential-file warning or risk acknowledgment

recommended_actions: In _add_read_only_file/_add (read path), detect gitignored credential-shaped files (.env, *.pem, *secret*) and require an explicit risk-acknowledgment confirm before adding them to context, per remit R-13.

findings.summary: The durable edit record is partial — commits and chat history are recorded, but there is no structured per-edit log of file and affected lines independent of git.
findings.description: The remit requires a durable local record of each edit including the file changed, the lines affected, and the commit id. Commit hashes are tracked (aider_commit_hashes) and chat history is appended, and analytics can write an event logfile, but no structured, action-level record of which lines were changed in each edit exists outside the git diff itself. When auto_commits is disabled or a commit fails, the line-level edit record the remit calls for is not durably captured.
findings.policy_rule_text: Aider MUST keep a durable local record of each edit — the file changed, the lines affected, and the commit identifier if committed.

evidence.snippet: show_auto_commit_outcome() records commit hashes in aider_commit_hashes; no separate structured per-edit (file + line range) audit record is written
optional event logfile captures event names/properties, not file/line-level edit detail sufficient to reconstruct each edit

recommended_actions: Write a structured, append-only edit log (file, line range, commit id) at each apply_edits/auto_commit so the line-level record survives independent of git state, per remit R-11.

findings.summary: No elevated approval gate distinguishes edits to security-sensitive files (CI/CD workflows, Dockerfiles, dependency manifests, CODEOWNERS) from ordinary edits.
findings.description: The remit requires human approval specifically for edits to security-sensitive files such as CI/CD workflow definitions, container build files, dependency manifests and lockfiles, and code-ownership files. allowed_to_edit() applies a single uniform confirm for files not yet in the chat and none at all for in-chat files; there is no path-class check that escalates approval for these high-impact files, so editing .github/workflows/*.yml is treated identically to editing any source file.
findings.policy_rule_text: Edits to security-sensitive files such as container build files, CI/CD workflow definitions, dependency manifests and lockfiles, code-ownership files, and security-policy files.

evidence.snippet: allowed_to_edit() — uniform confirm_ask for not-yet-added files and no gate for in-chat files; no security-sensitive-path classification anywhere (lines 2191-2240)

recommended_actions: Add a path-class check in allowed_to_edit() that requires an explicit elevated confirm for edits to CI/CD, container, dependency-manifest, and CODEOWNERS files, per remit R-19.

positives.title: Real human-in-the-loop confirmation gate
positives.description: confirm_ask() in io.py is a genuine, consistently-applied gate; shell-command execution uses explicit_yes_required=True so a bare Enter does not run commands, and it is invoked at file-add and shell-run sites.

positives.title: Shell execution requires explicit yes
positives.description: handle_shell_commands() gates every suggested shell command behind an explicit-yes confirm with a per-session ConfirmGroup, satisfying the remit's shell-confirmation requirement.

positives.title: Pinned dependencies with constraints files
positives.description: requirements.txt pins every dependency with == and references requirements/common-constraints.txt, giving a reproducible, version-locked supply chain.

positives.title: Edits to gitignored files are blocked
positives.description: allowed_to_edit() refuses to edit paths matching the gitignore spec, preventing inadvertent writes to ignored credential-bearing files.

rows.purpose: persists analytics uuid, opt-in status, and permanent-disable flag
append-only log of event name, properties, user_id, and timestamp when a logfile is configured
durable local record of the chat session including confirm prompts and responses

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Partial. Aider has one real, broadly-applied control — the developer confirmation gate — and good supply-chain hygiene (==-pinned requirements with constraints files), which lift Limit Your Domain and Manage Your Supply Chain to Established. But its declared boundaries are largely prompt- and convention-level rather than code-enforced: there is no repo-containment on file paths, no injection neutralization of repo content entering context, no secret scanning, and auto-commit/auto-lint run without a diff-accept gate. Zero Trust sits at Partial because the human gate is genuine but is the sole layer and is globally defeatable via --yes-always, and adversarial security testing is effectively absent despite a large functional test suite.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: Purpose-built coders and a repo-scoped index keep Aider on-task for local pair-programming, but abs_root_path()/safe_abs_path() in base_coder.py/utils.py apply no repo-containment, and --watch-files lets repo comment text expand the active task surface.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: Repository file contents, comments, docstrings and git history flow into LLM context with no injection-detection or neutralization step anywhere in the coders, despite the remit explicitly requiring such content be treated as untrusted and neutralized.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: confirm_ask() in io.py is a real human-in-the-loop gate enforced for shell execution (explicit_yes_required) and file-add, but auto_commit() commits without a diff-accept prompt, --yes-always collapses every gate globally, and there is no input validation or repo-containment on paths reaching the filesystem and LLM.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: requirements.txt pins every dependency with == and references constraints files (requirements/common-constraints.txt), a strong supply-chain posture; offset by hardcoded third-party analytics tokens (Mixpanel/PostHog) embedded in analytics.py.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: A substantial tests/ suite exists but it is functional regression testing of edit formats and git behavior; no adversarial prompt-injection, path-traversal, or secret-leak test artifacts or security-driven design changes were found in scope.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: analytics.py emits structured JSON events (and an optional append-only event logfile) and chat history is recorded, but telemetry is sampled/opt-in and event-level rather than a durable per-action audit trail of file edits and commits sufficient for incident reconstruction.
